name: Poo - Arenas Trail
name_original: Las Arenas de Cabrales a Poo de Cabrales — Parque Nacional Picos de Europa
description: Jog the moderate 3.2-mile (5.2 km) Poo - Arenas Trail in Picos de Europa National Park, gaining 312 feet (95 metres) with views of Cabrales
region_intro: Jogging routes around Cabrales are set within the Picos de Europa National Park, characterized by rugged mountains, deep gorges, and lush valleys. The region offers a diverse range of terrains, from challenging alpine paths to more accessible trails. This area provides a variety of running experiences amidst dramatic natural landscapes. Best jogging routes around Cabrales The most popular jogging route is Ring of Picos (3 Massifs), a 21.7 miles (35.0 km) difficult trail that takes 8 hours 43 minutes to complete, offering extensive views across the Picos de Europa. Another top favourite among local runners is Pico Cuetón from Arenas de Cabrales — circular in the Parque Nacional Picos de Europa, a difficult 14.7 miles (23.6 km) path. This route provides a challenging ascent with panoramic mountain vistas. Local runners also love the Picu Urriellu Circuit, a 14.6 miles (23.6 km) trail leading through the dramatic landscape surrounding Picu Urriellu, often completed in about 6 hours 2 minutes. Jogging around Cabrales is defined by rugged mountains, deep gorges, and lush valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Cabrales are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 800 runners have used komoot to explore Cabrales's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Cabrales?
A: Cabrales offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 45 trails available for exploration. These routes cater to various fitness levels, from easy paths to very challenging alpine runs.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging routes in Cabrales?
A: The jogging routes in Cabrales, set within the Picos de Europa National Park, feature incredibly diverse terrain. You'll encounter rugged mountains, deep gorges, and lush valleys. Options range from accessible paths and unpaved natural tracks to technical mountainous trails, ensuring a varied running experience.

Q: Are there any easy running routes for beginners in Cabrales?
A: Yes, while Cabrales is known for its challenging mountain trails, there are a few easier options available. The region offers 3 routes specifically rated as easy, perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed run. These typically involve less elevation gain and more stable surfaces.

Q: Can I find challenging trail running options in Cabrales?
A: Absolutely. Cabrales is a prime destination for challenging trail running. The area boasts over 30 difficult routes, many of which involve significant elevation changes and technical terrain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1229272651/ring-of-picos-3-massifs" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ring of Picos (3 Massifs)&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 21.7-mile trail offering extensive views across the Picos de Europa.

Q: Are there circular running routes in the Cabrales area?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Cabrales are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular circular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1228619721/pico-cueton-from-arenas-de-cabrales-circular-in-the-parque-nacional-picos-de-europa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Cuetón from Arenas de Cabrales&lt;/a&gt;, which provides a challenging ascent with panoramic mountain vistas.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while running in Cabrales?
A: Cabrales offers stunning natural features and landmarks. You can run past iconic peaks like Picu Urriellu, explore deep gorges, and discover traditional Asturian villages. Notable viewpoints include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/280873" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cable Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking vistas. You might also encounter various huts and refuges, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/175952" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vega de Urriellu Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, along your routes.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the iconic Picu Urriellu?
A: Yes, the dramatic Picu Urriellu (Naranjo de Bulnes) is a prominent feature in the Cabrales landscape, and several routes offer spectacular views of this natural monument. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1229204791/picu-urriellu-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picu Urriellu Circuit&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to lead through the stunning landscape surrounding this iconic peak.

Q: Is public transport available to access running routes in Cabrales?
A: Access to some trailheads in Cabrales, especially within the Picos de Europa National Park, can be limited by public transport. While major villages like Arenas de Cabrales have some bus services, reaching more remote starting points often requires a car. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for specific routes.

Q: Where can I park my car when accessing running trails in Cabrales?
A: Parking is generally available in and around the main villages such as Arenas de Cabrales and Sotres, which serve as common starting points for many routes. For more remote trailheads within the Picos de Europa National Park, designated parking areas may be available, but they can fill up quickly during peak season. Always check local regulations and signage.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Cabrales?
A: The best time for running in Cabrales is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally milder, and trails are more accessible. Winter can bring snow and ice, making many higher-altitude routes challenging or impassable, requiring specialized gear and experience.

Q: Can I find places to eat or drink near the running routes in Cabrales?
A: Yes, traditional Asturian villages like Arenas de Cabrales and Bulnes offer charming local establishments where you can find food and drink. Many routes pass through or near these villages, providing opportunities for a post-run meal or a refreshing drink. Some mountain refuges also offer basic provisions.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Cabrales?
A: The running trails in Cabrales are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.6 stars from numerous reviews. Runners frequently praise the breathtaking alpine scenery, the challenging yet rewarding terrain, and the immersive experience within the Picos de Europa National Park.
tips: Arenas de Cabrales is known worldwide as the birthplace of Cabrales cheese and is one of the most important access points to the Picos de Europa National Park.

The town is an authentic natural viewpoint of the impressive Naranjo de Bulnes. A good place to enjoy the panoramic views is from the Mirador del Pozo de la Oración, which is located just outside the town, towards Cangas, on the left. It is in a small recreational area where there is a picnic table.

Mirador del Picu Urriellu is a scenic viewpoint in Asturias. Mirador del Picu Urriellu is situated nearby to Viario de una roca viva, and close to Residencia de Mayores Ventana del Urriellu.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog the Poo - Arenas Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is designed for jogging and typically takes around 35-40 minutes to complete. This estimate is for a steady jogging pace, but your actual time may vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is the Poo - Arenas Trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, with a moderate difficulty rating and an elevation gain of about 95 meters over 5.19 kilometers, this route is suitable for beginners looking for a manageable jogging experience. It offers a good balance of challenge and accessibility.

faqs.question: Where does the Poo - Arenas Trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trail connects the villages of Arenas de Cabrales and Poo de Cabrales Village. You can start from either settlement. Parking is generally available in or near these villages, though it might be limited during peak seasons. It's advisable to check local signage for specific parking regulations.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for jogging on this route?
faqs.answer: As a jogging trail connecting two villages in the Picos de Europa National Park, you can expect a mix of paths, possibly including paved sections, gravel, or well-maintained dirt tracks. The moderate difficulty suggests some varied terrain, but it should be generally runnable.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Poo - Arenas Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail connects the charming villages of Arenas de Cabrales and Poo de Cabrales Village. A notable highlight along the way is the View of Picu Urriellu from Mirador del Picu Urriellu, offering stunning vistas of the iconic Picos de Europa landscape.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with other trails in the area. Notably, it covers 82% of the 'Poo - Arenas' official trail, 30% of 'Inguanzo - Poo', and 24% of 'Poo - Camarmeña - Poncebos'. You might also encounter parts of the 'Senda Fluvial Río Casaño' and 'Paseo de los Campeones. Travesera. Traveserina'.

faqs.question: Is the Poo - Arenas Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails within Picos de Europa National Park, but they must be kept on a leash at all times to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Always clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the Picos de Europa National Park?
faqs.answer: No, there is no general entrance fee or permit required to access the Picos de Europa National Park for day use, including jogging on trails like this one. However, specific activities or certain areas might have regulations, so it's always good to check local information if planning something beyond a standard jog.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog the Poo - Arenas Trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog this trail is typically during spring, early summer, and autumn. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Winter can be beautiful but may bring colder weather, snow, or ice, especially at higher elevations, which could make some sections more challenging.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or accommodation options near the trailheads?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Arenas de Cabrales and Poo de Cabrales Village are small settlements that offer various services. You can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options in both villages, making them convenient starting or ending points for your jog.

faqs.question: What should I wear and bring for jogging on this trail?
faqs.answer: For a moderate jogging trail, wear comfortable athletic clothing suitable for the weather, and appropriate jogging or trail running shoes. It's advisable to bring water, especially on warmer days, and perhaps a light jacket or rain shell as mountain weather can change quickly. Sun protection is also recommended.

waypoints.name: View of Picu Urriellu from Mirador del Picu Urriellu
